mnemonics.title: LOOP TYPES MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content:
FOR - FIXED ITERATIONS: Use FOR when you KNOW how many times.
WHILE - CONDITION FIRST: Use WHILE when condition comes BEFORE loop.
DO-WHILE - DO FIRST: Use DO-WHILE when you MUST run at least ONCE.

mnemonics.title: BREAK VS CONTINUE
mnemonics.content:
BREAK - BREAKS out completely, ends the loop entirely.
CONTINUE - CONTINUEs to next iteration, skips current one.
 mnemonic: BREAK = Break the prison (leave entirely), CONTINUE = Continue your journey (keep going).

mnemonics.title: FOR LOOP PARTS
mnemonics.content:
I.C.I. - INITIALIZE, CHECK, INCREMENT
Think: I Come In (to start the loop)
1. Initialize: Starting point
2. Condition: Running limit  
3. Increment: Step size

acronyms.usage: Loop that performs operation first, then repeats based on condition
Loop that continues WHILE condition is true

visualTips.visualization: Draw a horizontal line representing time. Mark points: START (initialization), CHECK (condition), DO (body), MOVE (increment), back to CHECK. Shows the flow: Initialize -> Check -> Body -> Increment -> Check again.

Draw two flowcharts side by side. WHILE: Box (condition) -> Diamond (check) -> Box (body) -> back to diamond. DO-WHILE: Box (body) -> Diamond (condition) -> back to body. Shows DO-WHILE always visits body at least once.
Imagine a clock: Outer loop = hours (0-11), Inner loop = minutes (0-59). For each hour, all 60 minutes complete. Total = 12 × 60 = 720 iterations.
Think of a race track. BREAK = takes exit ramp, leaves track completely. CONTINUE = skips current checkpoint but continues racing to next lap.

analogies.analogy: Like a countdown timer or a for loop is like reading pages of a book from page 1 to page n - you know exactly how many pages to read.
Like waiting for water to boil - you check the condition (boiling) repeatedly until it becomes true. Might check zero times if already boiling.
Like playing a game first and then checking if you want to continue - you always play at least once before deciding.
Like the rows and columns in a theater: Row 1 has seats 1-n, Row 2 has seats 1-n, etc. To visit all seats, you iterate through rows (outer) and for each row, iterate through seats (inner).
Like a revolving door that keeps spinning forever - it never reaches a stopping point unless someone physically stops it or it breaks.
